questions.stem: A 55-year-old asymptomatic man with type 2 diabetes and hypertension undergoes a routine abdominal ultrasound. The report describes a nodular liver edge, coarsened echotexture, and a small amount of splenomegaly (13 cm). LFTs are normal apart from a mildly elevated ALT (45 U/L) and GGT (60 U/L). Platelet count is 150 x 10^9/L. He drinks 2 units of alcohol per week. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The nodular liver edge and splenomegaly on imaging, along with mild transaminitis and low-normal platelets, are classic features of compensated cirrhosis. Simple steatosis would not cause nodularity or splenomegaly. Non-cirrhotic portal fibrosis typically presents with splenomegaly and varices without a nodular liver. PBC would show a high ALP. Haemochromatosis would have elevated ferritin and transferrin saturation.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with a 15-year history of excess alcohol intake (stopped 2 years ago) presents with fatigue and intermittent right upper quadrant pain. Examination reveals spider nevi, palmar erythema, and mild hepatomegaly. LFTs: ALT 60 U/L, AST 80 U/L, ALP 110 U/L, GGT 200 U/L, bilirubin 20 μmol/L, albumin 38 g/L. Platelet count is 120 x 10^9/L. An ultrasound shows a small, irregular liver with a nodular surface. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Stigmata of chronic liver disease (spider nevi, palmar erythema), low platelets, and ultrasound showing a shrunken, nodular liver are diagnostic of cirrhosis. The history of heavy alcohol use and elevated AST > ALT with high GGT point to an alcoholic aetiology. Alcoholic hepatitis would present acutely with jaundice and a much higher bilirubin. Chronic hepatitis C is possible but less likely given the alcohol history. PSC would show biliary dilatation, and acute hepatitis A is an acute illness.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with a BMI of 34, type 2 diabetes, and hypertension undergoes a CT scan for colorectal cancer screening. The scan shows an incidental nodular liver and borderline splenomegaly. He has no symptoms related to the liver. LFTs: ALT 35 U/L, AST 40 U/L, ALP 80 U/L, GGT 50 U/L, bilirubin 10 μmol/L, albumin 40 g/L. Platelet count is 160 x 10^9/L. He drinks no alcohol. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Compensated cirrhosis due to non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH)
questions.explanation: The combination of metabolic syndrome (obesity, diabetes, hypertension) with imaging evidence of cirrhosis (nodular liver, splenomegaly) and near-normal LFTs is typical of compensated NASH cirrhosis. Simple steatosis would not cause nodularity. There is no history of alcohol use or risk factors for hepatitis B. PBC would elevate ALP disproportionately.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man presents with progressive fatigue, bilateral knee arthritis, and skin hyperpigmentation over the past 6 months. He has no history of liver disease. Examination reveals hepatomegaly without splenomegaly. LFTs: ALT 30 U/L, AST 35 U/L, ALP 100 U/L, GGT 45 U/L, bilirubin 15 μmol/L. Ferritin is 1500 μg/L and transferrin saturation is 85%. An abdominal ultrasound shows a nodular liver with mild splenomegaly. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Compensated cirrhosis due to hereditary haemochromatosis
questions.explanation: Very high ferritin and transferrin saturation with skin pigmentation and arthritis are classic for hereditary haemochromatosis. The nodular liver on ultrasound confirms cirrhosis. While haemochromatosis alone can exist without cirrhosis, the imaging and splenomegaly suggest established cirrhosis. NASH would not show iron overload, PBC has high ALP, and rheumatoid arthritis does not cause nodular liver.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with a 10-year history of ulcerative colitis (now in remission) presents with fatigue and mild pruritus. He has no previous liver tests. Examination shows no stigmata of chronic liver disease. LFTs: ALT 40 U/L, AST 35 U/L, ALP 140 U/L, GGT 180 U/L, bilirubin 12 μmol/L, albumin 42 g/L. Platelet count is 135 x 10^9/L. An abdominal ultrasound reveals a nodular liver contour, mild splenomegaly (12.5 cm), but no biliary dilatation. MRCP is normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Compensated cirrhosis due to primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC)
Non-cirrhotic portal hypertension due to ulcerative colitis
questions.explanation: The association of ulcerative colitis with cholestatic liver biochemistry (ALP, GGT) suggests PSC. However, the nodular liver and low-normal platelets indicate cirrhosis, despite a normal MRCP (small-duct PSC can cause cirrhosis without visible strictures). PSC without cirrhosis would not show a nodular liver or splenomegaly. PBC is unlikely without AMA and has a different epidemiology. Autoimmune hepatitis typically shows transaminitis and high IgG. Non-cirrhotic portal hypertension is rare in UC.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old male engineer attends routine health screening. He feels well. Blood tests show platelets 110 ×10⁹/L, other FBC normal, LFTs normal. Abdominal ultrasound shows a slightly prominent spleen (13 cm). He drinks 20 units/week. What is the most appropriate next test to confirm or exclude cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Transient elastography is a validated non-invasive method to measure liver stiffness and diagnose cirrhosis. CT and MRI may show signs but are less sensitive for early cirrhosis. Endoscopy looks for varices but does not diagnose cirrhosis. Liver biopsy is invasive and not first-line.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and fatty liver on ultrasound. ALT 65 U/L. He drinks 10 units/week. What is the most appropriate initial risk stratification tool to rule out advanced fibrosis?
questions.explanation: NICE guideline NG49 recommends the NAFLD fibrosis score as the first non-invasive test to identify patients at low risk of advanced fibrosis. If indeterminate or high risk, transient elastography is then offered.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with known alcoholic liver disease has a FibroScan result of 22 kPa. Which of the following best describes this finding?
questions.explanation: FibroScan values >20 kPa correspond to F4 fibrosis (cirrhosis) in the Metavir system. This patient's stiffness of 22 kPa indicates established cirrhosis.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with positive anti-mitochondrial antibodies and raised ALP (1.5x ULN). She is asymptomatic. Ultrasound shows no obvious cirrhosis. What is the most appropriate non-invasive test to assess for cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: In primary biliary cholangitis, transient elastography is the first-line non-invasive test to stage fibrosis. Liver biopsy is reserved for inconclusive cases. MRCP and EUS evaluate the biliary tree but not fibrosis.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with BMI 30, drinks 14 units/week, has ALT 80 U/L, AST 60 U/L. She has no signs of chronic liver disease. What is the most appropriate first investigation to look for signs of chronic liver disease?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends abdominal ultrasound as the first-line imaging for abnormal LFTs to assess for steatosis, cirrhosis, or other pathology. Transient elastography is used later if ultrasound is suggestive or for fibrosis quantification.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old obese man (BMI 34) with known non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) is found to have compensated cirrhosis on liver biopsy. Upper GI endoscopy shows no varices. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Weight loss and lifestyle modification is the cornerstone of managing NASH cirrhosis. Vitamin E is only recommended for NASH without cirrhosis or diabetes, and pioglitazone is not indicated in cirrhosis. Ursodeoxycholic acid and metformin have no proven benefit in this setting.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with compensated cirrhosis secondary to primary biliary cholangitis (PBC) has a bilirubin of 30 μmol/L and elevated alkaline phosphatase. What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
questions.explanation: Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) is the first-line treatment for PBC, improving liver biochemistry and slowing progression. Obeticholic acid is reserved as second-line for inadequate response to UDCA. Corticosteroids and azathioprine are not indicated in PBC.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with compensated cirrhosis due to hepatitis C (genotype 3) is being evaluated for antiviral therapy. He has no varices, platelet count 150×10⁹/L, and albumin 35 g/L. Which of the following is CONTRAINDICATED?
questions.explanation: Peginterferon alpha is contraindicated in patients with compensated cirrhosis due to poor efficacy and high risk of side effects. Direct-acting antivirals (sofosbuvir/velpatasvir, glecaprevir/pibrentasvir) and daclatasvir are safe and effective. Ribavirin may be used cautiously but is not contraindicated.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with compensated cirrhosis due to hereditary haemochromatosis (C282Y homozygote) has ferritin 1200 ng/mL and transferrin saturation 85%. What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
questions.explanation: Phlebotomy is first-line therapy for hereditary haemochromatosis to reduce iron stores. Deferasirox and deferoxamine are iron chelators used for transfusion-related iron overload, not hereditary haemochromatosis. Vitamin C supplementation can increase iron absorption and is avoided. Dietary restriction alone is insufficient.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with compensated cirrhosis due to autoimmune hepatitis (AIH) started on prednisolone 20 mg daily and azathioprine 50 mg daily 2 weeks ago. She now presents with jaundice and a rise in bilirubin to 80 μmol/L. What is the most appropriate management?
questions.explanation: Azathioprine can cause cholestatic hepatitis, especially in the first few weeks of therapy. The most appropriate action is to stop azathioprine. Increasing prednisolone or adding other immunosuppressants is not indicated without excluding drug-induced liver injury. Liver biopsy may be considered if the diagnosis remains unclear, but initial management is to stop the suspected drug.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old male is found to have incidental cirrhosis on CT scan performed for a minor trauma. He has no history of liver disease, is asymptomatic, and his blood tests show normal bilirubin, normal INR, and normal albumin. He drinks minimal alcohol. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
questions.explanation: According to NICE, all patients with newly diagnosed cirrhosis should undergo OGD to screen for varices. Primary prophylaxis with NSBB (e.g., carvedilol) is only indicated if varices are found. Diuretics and transplant referral are not indicated in compensated cirrhosis without complications. Repeat imaging is not needed as an immediate step.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with known compensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh A) is admitted after a fall, with a fractured neck of femur. She has been taking ibuprofen 400mg three times daily for osteoarthritis. She is in pain and requests her usual medication. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Prescribe ibuprofen with a proton pump inhibitor
questions.explanation: NSAIDs are contraindicated in cirrhosis due to risk of gastrointestinal bleeding, renal impairment, and variceal bleeding. Paracetamol is safe at therapeutic doses (up to 4g/day) in stable cirrhosis. Although nerve block or morphine may be options, paracetamol is the safest first-line oral analgesia. NSAIDs should be avoided regardless of PPI cover. The immediate action is to prescribe a safe alternative.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with compensated alcoholic cirrhosis (abstinent for 3 years) is on carvedilol 6.25mg bd for primary prophylaxis of variceal bleeding. His last upper GI endoscopy 2 years ago showed no varices. Routine blood tests now show platelet count 95 x 10^9/L (previously 130). He is admitted for an elective hernia repair. What is the most appropriate next step during this admission?
options: Stop carvedilol due to low platelets
Request a repeat upper GI endoscopy before surgery
questions.explanation: A drop in platelet count below 120 (or presence of splenomegaly) is an indication to re-screen for varices per NICE. Since OGD was normal 2 years ago, a repeat OGD is appropriate before surgery to assess need for endoscopic bands or if beta-blocker is sufficient. Carvedilol dose is already adequate, and increasing it is not indicated without evidence of varices. Ultrasound may show splenomegaly but OGD is more direct.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with compensated cirrhosis due to non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) attends for her 6-monthly surveillance liver ultrasound. The ultrasound reports a new 2.5cm hypoechoic nodule in the right lobe with arterial hyperenhancement. She is asymptomatic. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Perform a diagnostic liver biopsy of the nodule
Arrange a contrast-enhanced CT scan of the liver
questions.explanation: The ultrasound findings are suspicious for hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). According to NICE, the diagnostic test of choice is a contrast-enhanced CT (or MRI) with arterial and portal venous phases. Biopsy is not first-line due to risk of seeding and false negatives. Treatment (sorafenib or transplant) depends on staging, which requires imaging. Repeat US would delay diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with compensated cirrhosis due to primary biliary cholangitis (PBC) is admitted with severe generalised pruritus that is disturbing her sleep and daily activities. She takes ursodeoxycholic acid 15mg/kg/day. Her liver function shows mild elevation of alkaline phosphatase but normal bilirubin and albumin. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
questions.explanation: For pruritus in PBC, NICE recommends colestyramine as first-line therapy. It binds pruritogens in the gut. Rifampicin is second-line due to risk of hepatotoxicity. Antihistamines are not effective in cholestatic pruritus. Increasing UDCA dose is not indicated. Transplant referral is premature without failure of medical therapy.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man is incidentally found to have cirrhosis on an abdominal ultrasound performed for dyspepsia. He is asymptomatic and has no previous liver history. On clinical examination, which single finding would most strongly indicate that he now has decompensated cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Shifting dullness indicates ascites, which is a hallmark of decompensated cirrhosis. The other options are stigmata of chronic liver disease that can occur in compensated disease.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with known compensated cirrhosis due to primary biliary cholangitis attends her routine hepatology clinic. She reports a new dull ache in the right upper quadrant over the past 3 weeks. She has lost 2 kg unintentionally. Which single diagnosis must be excluded?
questions.explanation: New right upper quadrant pain and weight loss in a cirrhotic patient are red flags for hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), requiring urgent imaging. Other options are possible but less likely and less immediately life-threatening.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with compensated alcoholic cirrhosis (no ascites, normal bilirubin) is brought to the emergency department after his wife noted increasing confusion over 2 days. He has not consumed alcohol for 6 months. On examination, which single neurological sign most strongly indicates hepatic encephalopathy?
questions.explanation: Asterixis is a classic sign of hepatic encephalopathy. Kayser-Fleischer rings suggest Wilson's disease. The others are not specific for HE.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with compensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh A) due to non-alcoholic steatohepatitis is seen in the emergency department after a fall. He has a small laceration on his forearm. His observations are normal. Which single laboratory value is most concerning for a coagulation defect requiring correction?
questions.explanation: An INR ≥1.5 in cirrhosis indicates significant synthetic dysfunction and increased bleeding risk. The other values are within normal ranges for cirrhosis.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with compensated cirrhosis due to hepatitis C (cured) is seen in clinic. He has no ascites and normal liver biochemistry. Which single physical finding on examination suggests the presence of significant portal hypertension and warrants endoscopic screening for varices?
questions.explanation: Caput medusae (abdominal wall collaterals) indicates portosystemic collateral circulation due to portal hypertension, making variceal screening urgent. Other stigmata are not specific for portal hypertension.
